words: home economics, it seems to me it was more mixed. I don't think it

speaker: MLL
words: necessarily was just freshmen that would take the beginning work. It

speaker: MLL
words: seems to me- I'm not real sure, I can't remember for sure about that.

speaker: MLL
words: Was there more than one class in home economics that you taught?

speaker: MLL
words: No, Ijust had one. It was just asmall school. And one year Ihad

speaker: MLL
words: some boys that wanted to take it, I think Ihad four boys. And they

speaker: MLL
words: were real good. They had to wear white aprons, too! (Chuckles)

speaker: SS
words: That's interesting.

speaker: MLL
words: They wore sort of butcher- type white aprons. Of course, boys are taking

speaker: MLL
words: it a lot nowdays.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think it courage in those days for boys to take it?

speaker: MLL
words: I don't exactly remember why they took it, but they wanted to and the

speaker: MLL
words: class wasn't filled up. It was such a small school that there weren't

speaker: MLL
words: always enough girls to make afull class, and so we let the boys take it

speaker: MLL
words: And they were real good at it. And of course, that doesn't hurt boys

speaker: MLL
words: any to learn to- they didn't do much sewing, but they did the cooking

speaker: MLL
words: part. Ican't remember much about the sewing part that I taught them.

speaker: MLL
words: I taught them some things about pressing clothes and darning and things

speaker: MLL
words: that boys would need to know, but clothing construction, I don t

speaker: MLL
words: believe they did much of that.

speaker: SS
words: Were you shocked when you first got there and saw that you didn't have

speaker: SS
words: any equipment to work with?

speaker: MLL
words: No, I don't remember that I was. I don't know whether I'd been prepared-

speaker: MLL
words: I can't remember that I was prepared for that exactly. But I'd never

speaker: MLL
words: seen a department where there wasn't anything. And I was always adven

speaker: MLL
words: turesome and I was willing to try. I taught there three years and then

speaker: MLL
words: I went to Omak and that was a much bigger school and they had had an

speaker: MLL
words: economics teacher but they didn't have much of a department either, and

speaker: MLL
words: they had me put in a good department. That was one of the things I

speaker: MLL
words: did while I was there. We started a real good-

speaker: SS
words: They gave—

speaker: MLL
words: Yes, equipped, well equipped. In this first instance t he school board

speaker: MLL
words: had to be convinced first, you see,it was important to put up any money

speaker: MLL
words: for anything. And I had to convince them that the electric sewing ma

speaker: MLL
words: chine was needed.

speaker: SS
words: Did you have to go to the board yourself?

speaker: MLL
words: Well, no. But then in a little town you soon got acquainted with them.

speaker: MLL
words: The depot agent was one of them, and we all got acquainted real quick!

speaker: MLL
words: And they knew the teachers, they saw to that that they got to know the

speaker: MLL
words: teachers. You know, through the church and through the outside acti

speaker: MLL
words: vities .

speaker: SS
words: You said when I was here before that the teacher in the community was

speaker: SS
words: very important.

speaker: MLL
words: They expected you to stay there and work! I di .rected the choir in

speaker: MLL
words: the church, extra. I'd always sung in church choirs, and that was fun,

speaker: MLL
words: too. And we had a quartet. The druggist was the tenor, the banker

speaker: MLL
words: was the bass and the superintendent's wife was a good soprano, and I

speaker: MLL
words: sang alto. And we had a honey a quartet and it was great fun.

speaker: SS
words: Where would you sing?

speaker: MLL
words: Oh, church. At the church. And sometimes other people would be in

speaker: MLL
words: the choir, too. but when you had a basic choir like a quartet, you

speaker: MLL
words: know, that we could all sing, it made it real interesting and nice.

speaker: MLL
words: The men loved it; this banker and druggist, they always came to prac

speaker: MLL
words: tise.

speaker: SS
words: Were there other activities that you were expected to take part in?

speaker: MLL
words: Well, there was PTA, and that was a bitter headache in those days.

speaker: SS
words: I didn't even know they had it.

speaker: MLL
words: Oh, yes, there was PTA and the teachers hated it, but it didn't amount

speaker: MLL
words: to very much as far as was concerned. It was more just a

speaker: MLL
words: social evening and teachers were bored to death. There was never any

speaker: MLL
words: issues or discussions of any account. It was mostly the parents- it

speaker: MLL
words: was a chance to meet the parents if they came. But we certainly didn't

speaker: MLL
words: like it we had to go.

speaker: SS
words: There were parties and that sort of thing- were you expected to go to

speaker: SS
words: them?

speaker: MLL
words: Well, there wasn't anything that you were expected to go to except

speaker: MLL
words: the high school parties. And they had public dances and they didn't

speaker: MLL
words: object to you going to those if you wanted to, but that wasn't- that

speaker: MLL
words: was your own business. If you wanted to join a lodge; there were 1 odges.

speaker: MLL
words: There was Rebecca Lodge. And we thought it was a good policy.

speaker: MLL
words: We joined; we didn't know anything about it, we were too young to be

speaker: MLL
words: good lodge members, but a couple of us did join it. And the Ku Klux

speaker: MLL
words: Klan was quite strong and they sent us invitations. We were scared not

speaker: MLL
words: to, and we were scared to-

speaker: SS
words: To what? To go?

speaker: MLL
words: To join. Ku Klux Klan was real strong at that time around. But it

speaker: MLL
words: was a two-sided proposition, you know. There were some people against

speaker: MLL
words: it and some for it, and we were afraid we'd lose our jobs if we didn't,

speaker: MLL
words: but we didn't. We never joined. But we got these invitations through

speaker: MLL
words: the mail, and we didn't know what to do. We didn't know- you don't

speaker: MLL
words: know who belongs 'We didn't know who to talk to about it and it was

speaker: MLL
words: real tricky, but we ended up not joining; this other teacher and I

speaker: MLL
words: that got invitations. Course now, Iwould know better how to deal with

speaker: MLL
words: athing like that, but then we just wanted our jobs awful bad, and we

speaker: MLL
words: were just going to or else.

speaker: MLL
words: But nothing ever happened? Nothing was ever said?

speaker: MLL
words: No. No. No, they never followed up and forced us to join or anything.

speaker: MLL
words: One person that I spoke to, that has really looked at it more in the

speaker: MLL
words: way that you were saying that you had to try to do what you had to do

speaker: MLL
words: to keep your job and to please them, and she made sound like it

speaker: MLL
words: was really rather difficult sometimes to do that. To keep them satisfied.

speaker: MLL
words: Inever had any trouble. Iliked all these people and Iliked all the

speaker: MLL
words: kids and we had fun. And Iwas too far from home- Ilived at Pullmanbut

speaker: MLL
words: it was too far in those days to go home except just for cations-

speaker: MLL
words: Christmas vacation; Thanksgiving. So there was no problem with me

speaker: MLL
words: staying there weekends, and it was fun to have things to , kids

speaker: MLL
words: would take us on picnics and things, you know. Ihad areal good time.

speaker: MLL
words: You didn't feel like your conduct was subject to too much inspection?

speaker: MLL
words: but there was no problem because I didn't We knew very well it was,

speaker: MLL
words: the things that they said you couldn't do. Ididn't smoke; not many

speaker: MLL
words: girls did smoke yet, but there were some and they.would have been out

speaker: MLL
words: if the school board knew they smoked. That wasn't allowed. But that

speaker: MLL
words: was no problem with me because Ididn't. And they didn't object to us

speaker: MLL
words: going to the public dances. Iboarded at aplace where there was quite

speaker: MLL
words: agang and we'd all go together. And they didn't object to that. I

speaker: MLL
words: did work in the church, which made me acceptable! (Chuckles) And they

speaker: MLL
words: liked it because I was used to tioing that. But they did- there were

speaker: MLL
words: things they would have censored you for.

speaker: SS
words: How would the situation compare say, to the school at Quincy or at

speaker: SS
words: Pullman in-

speaker: MLL
words: In that same time, you mean?

speaker: SS
words: Yes,

speaker: MML
words: Well, it compared-

speaker: SS
words: I mean when you were going to school.

speaker: MLL
words: Well, at Quincy, I think they didn't have home economics, but schools

speaker: MLL
words: about the same size- no, the Quincy school was smaller. The graduating

speaker: MLL
words: if class at Culdesac were bigger than my sisters and brothers had gone

speaker: MLL
words: through, but I was in a lower grade at that time. But the Pullman

speaker: MLL
words: school was so much bigger that there was no comparison, really to ac

speaker: MLL
words: tivities. The Culdesac had a strong athletic program, they'd hadbasketball

speaker: MLL
words: was ajbig, they didn't play football, but basketball was big.

speaker: MLL
words: And supported by the town and it had winning teams in the past, and

speaker: MLL
words: it was the important thing, as it still is in many schools. And they

speaker: MLL
words: had other activities; plays. Teachers had to take turns coaching plays.

speaker: MLL
words: And I never coached a play. The faculty put on a play once and we all

speaker: MLL
words: took part in it. But I never coached a play because I had this music

speaker: MLL
words: part. Let's see, did I have that just one year or two years? They

speaker: MLL
words: had mus|i£ teachers for the band and instruments, but it seems to me

speaker: MLL
words: that I had the glee club two years. And then we would put on skits

speaker: MLL
words: between acts of the plays, so that was my contribution, and I didn't

speaker: MLL
words: actually coach a play, but some of the teachers had to do that. And

speaker: MLL
words: programs for PTA, you had to help with those.

speaker: SS
words: They didn't have the, oh, kind of just get-togethers at school anymore

speaker: SS
words: there; this was more of a—

speaker: MLL
words: You mean parents and all?

speaker: SS
words: Where the kids and everybody came?

speaker: MLL
words: No. No. I don't think so. They had school parties; I don't think they

speaker: MLL
words: ever had school dances. I don't believe they danced but they had

speaker: MLL
words: school parties where you played games and things sometimes.

words: By the way, when you mentioned the Klan; do you remember any of them

speaker: SS
words: doing anything around there? Burning crosses and that sort of thing?

speaker: MLL
words: Yes, they burned crosses. Yeah, all through this country, they did

speaker: MLL
words: that up here, too, But I don't know of any bad things

speaker: MLL
words: they did, like we hear of in the South in the early days.

speaker: SS
words: I have heard of people around here that I mentioned the Klan and some

speaker: SS
words: of them mentioned that they were more anticatholic sometimes in some

speaker: SS
words: places.

speaker: MLL
words: Well, there wasn't any problem there. The town wasn't on the reserva

speaker: MLL
words: tion but the area around is the Indian Reservation, you know, but I

speaker: MLL
words: don't think that was an issue with the Klan either. I don't think In

speaker: MLL
words: dians were. That wasn't an issue down there. There weren't any Catho

speaker: MLL
words: lics there. There were Catholics at Lapwai, but not at Culdesac. There

speaker: MLL
words: were no Catholics or no church at Culdesac.

speaker: SS
words: Well, from there: when did you first become home demonstration

speaker: MLL
words: I think I was getting $135 at Culdesac, at the end, I think I got a

speaker: MLL
words: raise. I think I started out at $l25 a month, that was for nine months.

speaker: MLL
words: And I think I was getting $135 and I went to Omak at about $145, as I

speaker: MLL
words: remember, it wasn't a big raise, but it was and it was a big

speaker: MLL
words: ger school. And it was all working with girls; which is really what I

speaker: MLL
words: was best trained to do. I was girls advisor and then economics

speaker: MLL
words: classes and also taught girls' chemistry. And of course, I had had

speaker: MLL
words: chemistry.

speaker: SS
words: Did you board at Culdesac?

speaker: MLL
words: Yes. Teachers boarded, there were no apartments in those days.

speaker: SS
words: How about Omak?

speaker: MML
words: And the same at Omak; I boarded. I don't remember any teacher that

speaker: MML
words: had an apartment.

speaker: MML
words: MARVIN L:  What did you pay? About forty dollars a month?

speaker: MLL
words: Well, I paid thirty-five at Culdesac, I think. I can't remember at

speaker: MLL
words: Omak. was about the same, maybe forty a month.

speaker: SS
words: Did that include your breakfast and supper, too? As a part of that?

speaker: MLL
words: Three meals.

speaker: SS
words: Three meals a day?

speaker: MLL
words: Uh-huh. We lived close enough to the school. And your room.

speaker: SS
words: Was that pleasant? Boarding with a family?

speaker: MLL
words: Oh, yes, I lived with good families. Yes. I lived with a dentist's

speaker: MLL
words: family in Omak, and it was very pleasant. There were two other teachers

speaker: MLL
words: stayed there too. And we had washing privileges, you know. Do oor

speaker: MLL
words: laundry and pressing.Sudn ftS-4hd we were just like members of the family,

speaker: MLL
words: if we wanted to be. But the Omak school was much larger and progres

speaker: MLL
words: sive. Very progressive would say. They had a class for the boys that

speaker: MLL
words: was a combination of shop and agriculture, and then there was home eco

speaker: MLL
words: nomics for the girls and they had a girls' league, that I was the spon

speaker: MLL
words: sor of. And the superintendent was real interested in advancing that

speaker: MLL
words: work in the school. It wasn't vocational, home economics yet, at that

speaker: MLL
words: time, although we were qualified to teach vocational. We were trained

speaker: MLL
words: you see at Pullman so that we could teach vocational home economics,

speaker: MLL
words: but they didn't get federal help like we do now, or,, later. It wasn't

speaker: MLL
words: strictly vocational work; it was coming toward that. And with our trai

speaker: MLL
words: ning, teacher training and all, we were trained to teach vocational,

speaker: MLL
words: if we got into a school where they had it.

speaker: SS
words: You say Girls' League; what was that?

speaker: MLL
words: Well, it was all the girls in high school, and it was to teach- it was

speaker: MLL
words: an extracurricular group. And they had meetings, where they learned

speaker: MLL
words: to conduct- They had a business organization, you see, with officers

speaker: MLL
words: and they learned to do that. was part of my job to see that they

speaker: MLL
words: learned how to conduct meetings. I don't know if schools still have

speaker: MLL
words: it that way, I doubt if they do. But they also had a Student Body or

speaker: MLL
words: ganization, but usually it was boys that were the president and vice-president

speaker: MLL
words: and learned that part. The girls didn't learn that, but they

speaker: MLL
words: learned that in the Girls' League. And then they had dress codes- you

speaker: MLL
words: asked about that- and they did there. They had worked up what was ac

speaker: MLL
words: ceptable dress codes. And I don't remember now what some of the prob

speaker: MLL
words: lems were, but there were things that parents approved of that they

speaker: MLL
words: wanted in that and the girls worked- we worked that out with the girls.

speaker: MLL
words: And they accepted it, you know. Wasn't a uniform or anything.

speaker: MLL
words: It was just accepted ways of dressing. I taught there three years and

speaker: MLL
words: then/this superintendent had gone into the State Board. He was a real

speaker: MLL
words: fine man and he'd gone into the State office. And so we had a

speaker: MLL
words: good- the principal had moved up and he was good, too. But I decided

speaker: MLL
words: that was long enough for me there. And so this first superin

speaker: MLL
words: tendent I applied for a job at Highline High School on the coast,

speaker: MLL
words: And I went over and interviewed them and they gave me a contract, but it

speaker: MLL
words: included cafeteria work and I had no special training and I was a lit

speaker: MLL
words: tle afraid of the cafeteria. Highline was quite a big school, it's

speaker: MLL
words: between Tacoma and Seattle. It's real big now I imagine, but at that

speaker: MLL
words: time it much bigger than I was working in.

speaker: SS
words: What did cafeteria work

speaker: SS
words: I would have to manage the cafeteria, and I had never had any experience

speaker: SS
words: in that. We used to have a little school lunch at Culdesac where we

speaker: SS
words: made a kettle of soup and the youngsters washed the dishes and we ser

speaker: SS
words: ved them a bowl of soup or a cup of cocoa at noon with their sandwiches

speaker: SS
words: that they brought, you know. But that was easy. But managing a real

speaker: SS
words: cafeteria wasn't in my- exactly in my line and I was a little afraid of

speaker: SS
words: it, and I was hesitating over this contract before I signed it. It

speaker: SS
words: was a good job; real good job. And would have been quite a bit of

speaker: SS
words: teaching plus the cafeteria. And one day someone knocked on my class

speaker: SS
words: room door and it was the assistant director of extension at Pullman,

speaker: SS
words: and he called me out into the hall. He stood in the hall and he offered

speaker: SS
words: me a job in Wenatchee. Well, he couldn't offer me the job. I had to

speaker: SS
words: go down and interview the county agent first, but he wanted me to do

speaker: SS
words: that add then if the county agent liked me-My they wanted me to take

speaker: SS
words: this job. It was a brand new job, and that's how I got into extension

speaker: SS
words: work. Just like that! So, I went down on the bus- you could go down

speaker: SS
words: in the morning and come back on Saturday, you know. I went down and

speaker: SS
words: interviewed this county agent; they'd never had a home demonstration

speaker: SS
words: agent in Chelan County; it was in 1930, right in the start of the Dep

speaker: SS
words: ression. And he'd already organized a homemaker's council among

speaker: SS
words: the farm women and they were real anxious to get an agent, so I had

speaker: SS
words: this appointment with him on Saturday and I went to his office and

speaker: SS
words: he explained the shortcomings of the office; we'd have to share this

speaker: SS
words: room, which wasn't as big as this room, I don't think, and he also told

speaker: SS
words: me that he smoked cigars- Would I object to that?. And I said, "Hea

speaker: SS
words: vens no, I liked the smell of cigars." And he was quite relieved, be

speaker: SS
words: cause I think he felt that that had to be understood because I had to

speaker: SS
words: sit at a desk here and he sat at one here and we were practically backto-

speaker: SS
words: back, and he was not about to give up his cigars in the office.

speaker: SS
words: And that was one of the requirements. (Laughter)

speaker: SS
words: But, anyway, the in

speaker: SS
words: terview went like that, so then, I took the job and I liked him. And

speaker: SS
words: I knew nothing about it; nothing about the work. There was no state

speaker: SS
words: leader at that time to help me; a woman state leader, they were in be

speaker: SS
words: tween leaders and there was no one, and this county agent knew more about

speaker: SS
words: it than I did and he helped me get started. He had already or

speaker: SS
words: ganized the women. And so I worked through this Homemakers' Council.

speaker: SS
words: And Chelan County is a little different from any farm area in that it

speaker: SS
words: was strictly almost all orchards and no diversification at all. Rural

speaker: SS
words: people like on a wheat ranch usually have chickens and a cow and some

speaker: SS
words: pigs and sheep and could be somewhat selfsufficient, but the orchardists

speaker: SS
words: couldn't because all they raised was apples or fruits- different fruits.

speaker: SS
words: Most of them didn't even raise a garden.

speaker: SS
words: But they could have raised a garden-

speaker: MLL
words: They could have, but they didn't because apples had been so profitable

speaker: MLL
words: and they put all their time and effort into that. And then they hadat

speaker: MLL
words: that time the orchards were at bearing stage and they had all that

speaker: MLL
words: investment, and then this depression hit and there was no market and

speaker: MLL
words: they were in a tough spot. Because you see, an orchard wasn't even

speaker: MLL
words: like wheat land would be where you could let it lie fallow. It had to

speaker: MLL
words: be sprayed and pruned and fertilized to maintain it. And it was an ex

speaker: MLL
words: pensive business if you weren't getting a crop. So they were in a real

speaker: MLL
words: bad fix.

speaker: SS
words: Did they have any available land if they wanted to say, keep a pig or

speaker: SS
words: something like that?

speaker: MLL
words: Oh, they could- sure, they could have found a place for small diversi

speaker: MLL
words: fication, but most of them were- oh, a lot of 'em were college people,

speaker: MLL
words: you know that went over there and these orchards. Lots of the

speaker: MLL
words: women were- I had two home economics graduates among these ru ral wo

speaker: MLL
words: men that were on this council. But that had been- when they started

speaker: MLL
words: it was a profitable farming venture and it was just the Depression and

speaker: MLL
words: no market for their fruit was what was fouling the whole thing up.

speaker: MLL
words: And you see that was just before Franklin Roosevelt came in with his

speaker: MLL
words: big program that did save them then, of course. But it was real rough

speaker: MLL
words: for a year or two. I went there in the spring of '30, in June of '30

speaker: MLL
words: and he wasn't-

speaker: SS
words: '33, was when he came in; it was March of '33.

speaker: MLL
words: So it was at the end of my Working period is when those programs -

speaker: MLL
words: But we had some before that. We had WPA.

speaker: SS
words: I think the WPA came in just when Roosevelt did. I think Hoover didn't

speaker: SS
words: do anything.

speaker: MLL
words: Wasn't it '32 instead of '33? Well, anyway, it was June of '30 that

speaker: MLL
words: I went there. Really, if you want to know all about this work that I

speaker: MLL
words: did-

speaker: SS
words: Yes, I do.

speaker: MLL
words: There was this work trying to do something for these people that had

speaker: MLL
words: these orchards that were well educated, but were stuck with all this

speaker: MLL
words: expense, you see, of maintaining these orchards, and,the women had no

speaker: MLL
words: money to spend and so we set up nutrition programs and tried to teach

speaker: MLL
words: them things that they could do to help. And gardens were important,

speaker: MLL
words: but the county agent had charge of gardens, so we tried to get them to

speaker: MLL
words: raise gardens, and they did, they put in gardens. And that helped their

speaker: MLL
words: food budget. And a few of them tried cows, milk cow, you know, and

speaker: MLL
words: then the Red Cross, there was Red Cross flour and a lot of those people

speaker: MLL
words: that I've just described were dependent on getting free Ree Cross flour.

speaker: MLL
words: And then we'd have baking programs. Of course, most of them knew how

speaker: MLL
words: to cook, so that part wasn't important with them. But we had a big

speaker: MLL
words: canning program.

speaker: MLL
words: They'd even got out of the habit of that because they'd made enough

speaker: MLL
words: money- and we had a big canning program and we worked with drying and

speaker: MLL
words: went back to some of the pioneer customs. We made soap; taught them

speaker: MLL
words: to make soap from their waste fats. And a lot of remodeling work in

speaker: MLL
words: clothing; we did a lot on mending and remodeling and making over. Wor

speaker: MLL
words: king in homefurnishings because their homes had gotten rundown. Some

speaker: MLL
words: of them had nice homes but then without maintenance, they were rundown.

speaker: MLL
words: We worked on rugs and hooked and braided rugs made from rags, you know.

speaker: MLL
words: And studied, along with the making, the technique of making the wo

speaker: MLL
words: men knew how and I learned. I didn't know much about that at the time.

speaker: MLL
words: We did refinishing of old furniture and caned chairs. The 4-H girls

speaker: MLL
words: had all these same projects, you see, we worked through the youth into

speaker: MLL
words: some of the families, and then we worked with the women, too. I had

speaker: MLL
words: one of these clubs that I worked with. But you could teach the girls

speaker: MLL
words: that came out in the home, you see, they would take their rooms as a

speaker: MLL
words: a project and they would try to do it without any expense, or very lit

speaker: MLL
words: tle expense.

speaker: MLL
words: We made dressing tables out of orange crates, for insstance.

speaker: MLL
words: Orange crates you could get for nothing at the grocery store,

speaker: MLL
words: and putting orange crates, they would have a shelf in them, with

speaker: MLL
words: a board between, make a little desk or a little dressing table, and

speaker: MLL
words: then they would cover that with a flounce or sametimes they painted

speaker: MLL
words: them and made little desks and that sort of thing that they could do

speaker: MLL
words: that made attractive rooms. And then 4-H work was quite highly organ

speaker: MLL
words: ized by then, and they had contests where they put on demonstrations

speaker: MLL
words: at the fairs, and our little local fair just in the county. And the

speaker: MLL
words: would work up a demonstration how to make a dressing table out

speaker: MLL
words: of orange crates, see, and that was a way of teaching. Demonstrations

speaker: MLL
words: were used for teaching

speaker: SS
words: Were you directing the 4-H work?

speaker: MLL
words: I was directing - just the county agent and I did the whole thing. He

speaker: MLL
words: did the gardens and the livestock and certified potatoes with the boys

speaker: MLL
words: and that sort of thing and I did the girls' work, The girls in gar

speaker: MLL
words: dening were under him, but they had homefurnishing projects; room im-

speaker: MLL
words: provement, we called it,for the girls. And canning projects. The girl

speaker: MLL
words: would choose what project she wanted for that year. And they had cooking

speaker: MLL
words: projects and they had sewing projects. And in the sewing projects,

speaker: MLL
words: again, we tried to teach the girls to make over, and they learned simple

speaker: MLL
words: stitches to start with. They hemmed a dishtowel, for instance. We 11,

speaker: MLL
words: the dishtowel was a flour sack or sugar sack, and they learned their

speaker: MLL
words: hemming on that and then they made simple articles. The young ones

speaker: MLL
words: did, you know. Potholders and then they advanced to clothing, and

speaker: MLL
words: they had style shows finally. And the advanced girls would make ela

speaker: MLL
words: borate costumes, and then they would exhibit these at the fairs. Well,

speaker: MLL
words: one girl one year- a sugar sack was different from a flour sac k- it

speaker: MLL
words: was a larger piece of fabric; this was a hundred-pound sugar sack that

speaker: MLL
words: people bought in those days, and it was a finer fabric and more open

speaker: MLL
words: weave in a way, but it was a finer fabric, and this girl took these

speaker: MLL
words: sugar sacks and bleached them and starched them a bit, and made a cos

speaker: MLL
words: tume, a complete costume out of sugar sacks that she exhibited at the

speaker: MLL
words: fair and it was a winning exhibit and it was loaned to the state clo

speaker: MLL
words: thing specialist who took it all around the state and showed it as an

speaker: MLL
words: example of what a girl had done with nothing. Something that didn't

speaker: MLL
words: cost her anything. It looked like a white linen costume when she had

speaker: MLL
words: it finished. And she made the slip and the panties and the bra, the

speaker: MLL
words: whole thing out of these same material. That was just one example that

speaker: MLL
words: they all did- they all had to make garments and there were certain re

speaker: MLL
words: gulations about how they would be made.

speaker: SS
words: Were all the people that you worked with quite motivated by the diffi

speaker: SS
words: cult times to cut any corner they possibly could?

speaker: SS
words: The girls and the women?

speaker: MLL
words: Yes. They were from the rural families that lived - mostly. But in

speaker: MLL
words: the county there was sections that were- where they were more- where

speaker: MLL
words: they had less. .The farms were not as productive. And those families

speaker: MLL
words: then didn't have as much to work with as the others did. And there

speaker: MLL
words: were two sections; south of Wenatchee the soil wasn't as good so it

speaker: MLL
words: didn't produce the same, and so there was a little different problem

speaker: MLL
words: there. But most of those families already were more diversified and

speaker: MLL
words: that helped, too. But then in Wenatchee- I worked in the whole count you

speaker: MLL
words: see we were paid- extension work was cooperative- it was federal-

speaker: MLL
words: Department of Agriculture and the college and the county. It was a

speaker: MLL
words: three way contribution our salaries. We worked for all three, and

speaker: MLL
words: so we had to work with all people in the county if they wanted us to.

speaker: MLL
words: And in Wenatchee at that time, there were three classes of people; the

speaker: MLL
words: ones I've been talking about were the ones who owned orchards, and then

speaker: MLL
words: in the city of Wenatchee, which was a big town at that time, a big town

speaker: MLL
words: there were people that was the laboring class were down and out

speaker: MLL
words: because there was no employment.

speaker: MLL
words: And so we tried to work with- the

speaker: MLL
words: county had to put up money for them. The county commissioners had to

speaker: MLL
words: put up money for them to buy groceries with, so they were anxious for

speaker: MLL
words: us to help them, so that that would reduce their load there. And s o

speaker: MLL
words: we set up gardens on vacant lots in Wenatchee. That was the county

speaker: MLL
words: agent's job, he did that, and then I worked with the women on the nu

speaker: MLL
words: tritional problems; tried to, using this free Red Cross flour that we

speaker: MLL
words: gave out through our office and tried to teach them to- Well, the fact

speaker: MLL
words: No. I'm talking about these people that owned these orchard snow.

speaker: MLL
words: they were in those circumstances meant that they were not too ambitious

speaker: MLL
words: to start with, you see and a lot of them didn't know how to bake bread.

speaker: MLL
words: Some of them didn't, and so we had a program on baking bread. And some

speaker: MLL
words: of them could get wheat, and they used quite a bit of wheat and would

speaker: MLL
words: grind it or would cook it and make casseroles and all. Unground wheat

speaker: MLL
words: if it's cooked long enough becomes very palatable. And sometimes they

speaker: MLL
words: could get that cheaper than they could things they bought at the store.

speaker: MLL
words: And so we worked with that. We did some canning, but they didn't have

speaker: MLL
words: equipment. At that time we weren't canning vegetables in waterbath,

speaker: MLL
words: and it was vegetables that were the problem and we tried some drying.

speaker: MLL
words: The climate was very favorable to drying, because Wenatchee is hothas

speaker: MLL
words: a long, hot summer. And we did drying of fruits and also some vegtables.

speaker: MLL
words: Green beans can be dried, but I never thought it was worthwhile.

speaker: MLL
words: We had directions for it, but they never taste very good after they are

speaker: MLL
words: dried, but corn of course, is excellent. And most of the fruits. And

speaker: MLL
words: some of them made dryers that they used on their stoves or dried in the

speaker: MLL
words: oven, and most of them did some drying. They did some of that. And

speaker: MLL
words: then we did some work on clothing; remodeling, that would help them in

speaker: MLL
words: making over things to be in circumstances a little better. I didn't

speaker: MLL
words: do much homefurnishing work for these people; their homes were very

speaker: MLL
words: bleak, but they didn't have- Well, a lot of them probably came there to

speaker: MLL
words: work in the fruit and then you see when there was no market there wasn't

speaker: MLL
words: that work in the warehouses and in the orchards that there had been, so

speaker: MLL
words: they were out of work.

speaker: SS
words: How did you organize that? Did you give classes, or what?

speaker: MLL
words: Well, we tried to do it through neighborhood groups. We never had clubs.

speaker: MLL
words: They weren't the kind of women that belonged to clubs of any kind.

speaker: MLL
words: These other women had organizations-^ worked through the organizations

speaker: MLL
words: they already had quite often. Like Ladies' Aides or Grange groups or

speaker: MLL
words: different kinds of clubs that they'd already organized, that they'd

speaker: MLL
words: always had. And they would invite me to work with them, and once a

speaker: MLL
words: month I would have a program with them. But this other group in town

speaker: MLL
words: didn't have that kind of social organization and so we would have a

speaker: MLL
words: list of people that the county was providing groceries to and we would

speaker: MLL
words: work through that and get them together in neighborhood groups on a

speaker: MLL
words: small scale. I wouldn't say it was too successful, but then, as I told

speaker: MLL
words: you about these high school students, you don't know what was accom

speaker: MLL
words: plished, you don't really know, and hopefully we helped some of them.

speaker: SS
words: Were these people, many of them, do you think were really the migrant

speaker: SS
words: labor force?

speaker: MLL
words: Well, some of them probably were, but then there was a third group I

speaker: MLL
words: haven't told you about yet that was worse yet. And there was a place

speaker: MLL
words: down along the Columbia River, just at the edge of town, across the

speaker: MLL
words: tracks that was called'jungles'. And most towns had jungles in those

speaker: MLL
words: days. They were mostly the tramps that rode the boxcars in there, but

speaker: MLL
words: in Wenatchee a lot of these migrant workers had been caught without

speaker: MLL
words: anything and they were living down there in any kind of a little shack.

speaker: MLL
words: They couldn't get away, they didn't have any money to leave . with

speaker: MLL
words: so they were caught there for the winter. And they hadn't earned enough

speaker: MLL
words: to support themselves, and they were just existing down there as best

speaker: MLL
words: they could. There were also tramps. And it wasn't a very pleasant

speaker: MLL
words: area to try to do anything in. A lot of those little, shacks were built

speaker: MLL
words: out of cardboard and corrugated metal for a roof and just mak eshift

speaker: MLL
words: accommodations. The furnishings were very meager, but the county com

speaker: MLL
words: missioners wanted me to go down there and see what I could do because

speaker: MLL
words: they couldn't let these people starve, but the chief of police in Wena

speaker: MLL
words: tehee wouldn't let me go alone, even in that day. I wouldn't have been

speaker: MLL
words: afraid; I didn't know enough to be afraid, but he said, "No, I'll go

speaker: MLL
words: with you." And he always went with me. So that was something.

speaker: SS
words: What did you do down there?

speaker: MLL
words: Well, I tried to teach them- for instance they could have this free

speaker: MLL
words: flour, but they had such meager equipment. We tried to teach them to

speaker: MLL
words: bake bread, and some of them had stoves, you know, old stoves were

speaker: MLL
words: thrown out in those days and they could pick up an old st ove and some

speaker: MLL
words: of them had ovens and we did some work on bread baking with this free

speaker: MLL
words: flour and taught them to make their own yeast. You know, you can get

speaker: MLL
words: yeast started and then you can keep a starter going and so you save

speaker: MLL
words: the expense of yeast. That was one thing I taught them, but I don't

speaker: MLL
words: know how many of them— you couldn't get results from that work very

speaker: MLL
words: much, so I don't know how many of them ever used it. But that was some

speaker: MLL
words: thing you did. We taught them- they didn't have equipment to do much

speaker: MLL
words: canning, but we taught them what we could and drying. They could dry

speaker: MLL
words: things. And there were plenty of apples available because apples were

speaker: MLL
words: being dumped, you know, there was no market, so that they could have

speaker: MLL
words: all the apples. In fact, the orchardists would bring apples to needy

speaker: MLL
words: people like that, because they just dumped them out in a pile and that

speaker: MLL
words: didn't help the contamination of the orchards any; the apples dumped,

speaker: MLL
words: you know, because the codling moth then would flourish. But we worked

speaker: MLL
words: with this group down there in the jungles. Very discouraging.

speaker: SS
words: Families?

speaker: MLL
words: Families, yes, families, yes.

speaker: SS
words: What kind of condition were the kids in?

speaker: MLL
words: Well,not very good nutritionally, of course, because they weren't get

speaker: MLL
words: ting a well balanced diet. The county was giving them a little order

speaker: MLL
words: I don't remember what it was they gave them, so that they existed, but

speaker: MLL
words: it wasn't a very well balanced diet. But if they could help themselves

speaker: MLL
words: a little, do some cooking, you know, that helped.

speaker: SS
words: Did those people seem defeated? Just defeated or desperate?

speaker: MLL
words: Well, they were desperate. Living down there that was- and of course,

speaker: MLL
words: I don^t know what happened to them. I suppose as times got betterwell,

speaker: MLL
words: those programs came in they got work; WPA and those work pro

speaker: MLL
words: grams, you know, and that saved 'em. But there was a period in there

speaker: MLL
words: when it was pretty bleak.

speaker: SS
words: Were they there during the whole time you were there? Did they stay

speaker: SS
words: through '31?

speaker: MLL
words: Yeah, I didn't work with them- I don't think I worked with them but

speaker: MLL
words: one year. That first year I didn't work with them, I didn't get star

speaker: MLL
words: ted that well, but it must have been in '31 and '32. I left there and

speaker: MLL
words: went into the state office in '34, '35. It was after Roosevelt had

speaker: MLL
words: all these pltegrams going, because our county agent was real involved

speaker: MLL
words: with AAA and all this stuff.

speaker: SS
words: Were those people often arrested in Wenatchee at that time?

speaker: MLL
words: You mean these that lived down in the jungle?

speaker: SS
words: Yeah.

speaker: MLL
words: I don't know. I don't remember about them. You mean stealing and all

speaker: MLL
words: that? No, I don't remember that. I don't think so.

speaker: MLL
words: MARVIN L:  They just chased 'em out from one place to another.

speaker: MLL
words: These people were allowed to stay there, there was no place for them

speaker: MLL
words: to go.

speaker: SS
words: At that time?

speaker: MLL
words: Uh-huh. They did some places, and later they did clean out all those

speaker: MLL
words: jungles, and I imagine they cleaned that one out too. When there was

speaker: MLL
words: work available, but at that time there was just nothing anywhere.

speaker: SS
words: Were these mostly just- really cardboard? They really built houses

speaker: SS
words: out of cardboard?

speaker: MLL
words: Yes. Say Kellogg Cornflakes, or it would say whatever the box- the

speaker: MLL
words: big carton was that they used to create the- had to have a wood frame

speaker: MLL
words: of some kind to nail them to, but they would- some of them were just

speaker: MLL
words: hovels, really. But some of them were shacks; wooden shacks. I ex

speaker: MLL
words: pect that had been there a long time, that jungle but the itinerantan

speaker: MLL
words: industry like that attracted itinerant people, you see.

speaker: SS
words: It would have more permance than say the jungle they had here in Kendrick.

speaker: MLL
words: Well, here there were no buildings. Here it was just a place where

speaker: MLL
words: they had a pot over the fire^by the railroad tracks when they stayed

speaker: MLL
words: between trains or something.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think they were sharing their food with each other?

speaker: MLL
words: Well, I expect some of them did, and probably some of them fought, I

speaker: MLL
words: don't know, normal. But I didn't know anything about that part

speaker: MLL
words: of it. I didn't know about that. I just know that I was assigned

speaker: MLL
words: this job to go down there- and it was a hopeless assignment to begin

speaker: MLL
words: with; but we tried.

speaker: SS
words: What about these working people, the middle group you talked about who

speaker: SS
words: were not doing well. What was their attitude about what was going on

speaker: SS
words: that you could tell.

speaker: MLL
words: Oh, you mean the ones that lived in homes up here in town?

speaker: SS
words: Yes.

speaker: MLL
words: They were discouraged. They were discouraged.

speaker: MLL
words: MARVIN L:  They got food orders,

speaker: MLL
words: Yes, they got small food orders, but they were real discouraged, be

speaker: MLL
words: cause most of them wanted to work and couldn't get work, you know.

speaker: MLL
words: And that's the difference between the ones that wanted to work and

speaker: MLL
words: the ones that-

speaker: MLL
words: MARVIN L:  Fooi orders.

speaker: MLL
words: And they had homes. I don't know how they hung onto those little hou

speaker: MLL
words: ses. They didn't live in the good houses of the town. And then there

speaker: MLL
words: were a lot of people that had good jobs. We had good jobs. We were

speaker: MLL
words: getting our salary all the time. And the business people of Wenatchee

speaker: MLL
words: were making it alright. They had good stores and all, but of course

speaker: MLL
words: the economy did depend on the orchards.

speaker: MLL
words: MARVIN L:  The worst of it were the transients.

speaker: SS
words: The transient what?

speaker: SS
words: MARVIN L:  Transients.

speaker: MLL
words: So many came in- well they were migrant workers, they came in to work

speaker: MLL
words: in the fruit. They were trained to sort- you know the whole valley-

speaker: MLL
words: I worked in the whole county, Chelan and Entiat and Leavenworth., All

speaker: MLL
words: those towns had big warehouses, see it was all orchard country. And

speaker: MLL
words: then I worked with a few groups out on the fringe, up toward Lake Wena

speaker: MLL
words: tchee there was a group of women that I worked with in the winter whose

speaker: MLL
words: husbands worked in the woods or worked on the highway and they weren't

speaker: MLL
words: orchardists. And they didn't have time in the summer for meetings and

speaker: MLL
words: I would go up there in the winter. And the snow was deep in that coun

speaker: MLL
words: try above Leavenworth up around Lake Wenatchee. And I had a group up

speaker: MLL
words: there and I'd go up there in February usually. And now, I'm a pansy

speaker: MLL
words: about driving, but I had a car; we never put on chains unless we got

speaker: MLL
words: stuck; we never had winter tires and the snowbanks were way up higher

speaker: MLL
words: than the car along both sides of the roads. They'd just plow out the

speaker: MLL
words: road and I would drive up there and in parking, it was a bit hazardous

speaker: MLL
words: but then the men were always home because they didn't work at that time

speaker: MLL
words: at whatever their work was, it was some kind of logging, and so then

speaker: MLL
words: when I couldn't get out, when I couldn't back my car out they'd come

speaker: MLL
words: and push me out. I can remember that happening many times. I didn't

speaker: MLL
words: even haye to put on chains to get out of that snowbank, and then I could

speaker: MLL
words: drive back to Wenatchee without any chains. (Chuckles) Oh, now, I'd

speaker: MLL
words: be scared to death.

speaker: SS
words: Were there people who were down and out like these shack people in

speaker: SS
words: other places besides Wenatchee?

speaker: MLL
words: I never worked with any. I don't . there were. I think they were

speaker: MLL
words: kind of concentrated down there.

speaker: MLL
words: MARVIN L:  Wherever there were transient workers.

speaker: MLL
words: The county did furnish.

speaker: SS
words: Were they very receptive to what you were trying to do? Or not?

speaker: MLL
words: Well, I don't know. I really don't know. I never could tell. They

speaker: MLL
words: were friendly. And they knew I worked for the county and they knew

speaker: MLL
words: the county was feeding them and so they did cooperate. And I don't

speaker: MLL
words: know- you know it's hard to know why they did or if they really used

speaker: MLL
words: much of it. But the county commissioners wanted us to do this and

speaker: MLL
words: these people were getting the county orders and they knew they'd bet

speaker: MLL
words: ter- it was partly that kind of an attitude, I think. But the n most

speaker: MLL
words: of them were interested in trying to better themselves if they could.

speaker: MLL
words: I never got too well acquainted with them because I didn't work with

speaker: MLL
words: them the same as I did the other rural people. My work was mainly with

speaker: MLL
words: the rural people; strictly rural people that lived on orchards tractsfarms

speaker: SS
words: These people: you say there was a council that was set up before you

speaker: SS
words: got there.

speaker: MLL
words: They invited somebody from each community. They invited women,the

speaker: MLL
words: leading- he knew them, he'd been there quite a while, the county agent

speaker: MLL
words: had, and he invited these leading women. He picked them out and invi

speaker: MLL
words: ted them to a meeting and then they set up their council and elected

speaker: MLL
words: officers and that was all done before I got there. And the woman who

speaker: MLL
words: was president, she was a home economics graduate. Some mideast college

speaker: MLL
words: I've forgotten now- But their home- they had a very attractive house

speaker: MLL
words: but was in badly need of money to repair and refurnish and they were

speaker: MLL
words: raising a family and so forth, so they were in bad circumstances, but

speaker: MLL
words: she was a good leader. When I went back- they had a thirty year cele

speaker: MLL
words: bration and invited me back, and this same woman was planning a trip

speaker: MLL
words: to Europe. Those that could hang onto their orchards snapped out of

speaker: MLL
words: it and became well-off. And she was still working at it, thirty years

speaker: MLL
words: later. Prosperity had come to the fruit business.

speaker: SS
words: At that time were most of them struggling to hang onto their orchards?

speaker: MLL
words: Well, yes, because of the expense. Some of them pulled out their

speaker: MLL
words: trees because they couldn't leave them and let the disease take overfruit

speaker: MLL
words: diseases, codling moth and all, they couldn't do that. And if

speaker: MLL
words: they raised apples they had to be picked and disposed of, and so th ere

speaker: MLL
words: was a lot of expense and they had to be pruned and they had to be fer

speaker: MLL
words: tilized and they had to be sprayed. And the spraying was a big, ex

speaker: MLL
words: pensive program. And some of them did pull out their orchards. But

speaker: MLL
words: on the main, they came through. There were government loans that came

speaker: MLL
words: in pretty fast that helped them and all these different programs.

speaker: SS
words: What was their attitude towards your work? Were they very positive?

speaker: SS
words: These local rural women?

speaker: MLL
words: Oh, yes. They were real interested in it and they made good contributions,

speaker: MLL
words: too. We used them in the program. A woman knew how to do

speaker: MLL
words: something we would let her teach the others, you know. That's the way

speaker: MLL
words: extension work functions.

speaker: SS
words: Are you talking now about the council women or just the women every

speaker: SS
words: place you went, like the Ladies' Aid?

speaker: MLL
words: In the clubs, well, it was the council women - but I went out and met

speaker: MLL
words: with these clubs and then there would be other women that would know

speaker: MLL
words: how to do something real well that would assist with the teaching of

speaker: MLL
words: that theme. There were a lot of things that I wasn't skillful at that

speaker: MLL
words: I needed their help. We had a big rug show, and I knew nothing about

speaker: MLL
words: rugs, but I learned, I got in and studied and did learn some. But some

speaker: MLL
words: of them knew how to make hooked rugs and braided rugs and I used them

speaker: MLL
words: as teachers in it. We had a big show and that was something nice they

speaker: MLL
words: could make for their homes that didn't cost them anything, they could

speaker: MLL
words: use burlap sack and rags and create something new for their home, it

speaker: MLL
words: gave them a little boost, you know. And we had a big show. And one

speaker: MLL
words: time we had a show and then we studied other things that they had along

speaker: MLL
words: with out homefurnishing programs. Some of them would have things tuc

speaker: MLL
words: ked away, old quilts or old handwoven coverlets or something. One year

speaker: MLL
words: we had a display at one of our meetings; we had a display of handwoven

speaker: MLL
words: coverlets.

speaker: MLL
words: We tried to make the program interesting to them, any

speaker: MLL
words: body that had one would bring it and display it, and some of them were

speaker: MLL
words: very old and very beautiful, you know. And then we had little apron

speaker: MLL
words: contests along with it; that was the practical part of it. And we had

speaker: MLL
words: Na kit of patterns we passed around and that was something new they

speaker: MLL
words: could make and an apron didn't cost much to make. And then we had a

speaker: MLL
words: contest and we invited Prudence Penny, who was the home economics wo

speaker: MLL
words: man for the Seattle Post-Intelligencer. And Prudence Penny was her

speaker: MLL
words: name on the paper, you know. She's probably still there, I don't know

speaker: MLL
words: but it was different women at different times like Betty Crocker is.

speaker: MLL
